name: star98
description: 1998 California STAR testing results: 9th-grade math scores for 303 school districts, used as a classic textbook example of weighted least squares regression.
source: Jeff Gill, "Generalized Linear Models: A Unified Approach" (Sage, 2000); bundled and redistributed via statsmodels.datasets.star98
copyright_note: statsmodels bundles this dataset "with express permission from the original author, who retains all rights" - that permission was granted to statsmodels specifically, not explicitly to further redistribution. Accepted here as low practical risk given the dataset's ~15 years of unchallenged redistribution via statsmodels (BSD-licensed, widely installed), but flagging the ambiguity rather than treating it as clearly resolved.

target.name: pass_rate_pct
target.description: Percentage of 9th graders scoring above the national median on the math section.

weight.description: Total number of 9th graders tested per school district. Larger MATHTOT means a statistically more reliable pass-rate estimate (variance of a proportion scales as ~1/n), so it is the natural precision weight - the classic textbook rationale for weighted least squares on this exact dataset.

validated.method: Compared plain OLS vs WLS (numpy weighted least squares) and pyoperon SymbolicRegressor unweighted vs sample_weight=MATHTOT fits.
validated.result: Each fit wins on its own objective: the unweighted-trained model has lower unweighted MSE, the weighted-trained model has lower weighted MSE - confirming sample_weight has the expected real-world effect on genuine data, not just a synthetic scenario.
